title: The 134% Yield Trap: Why ULTY and Ultra-High-Yield ETFs Are Bleeding Investors Dry
description: ULTY pays a 134% yield but distributions have collapsed 63% and NAV keeps eroding. Here's how to spot yield traps before they destroy your portfolio.
lede: The YieldMax Ultra Option Income Strategy ETF (ULTY) advertises a 134% dividend yield — paying $40.20 per share annually on a $30 stock. Those numbers should make any investor suspicious. And for good reason: ULTY's distributions have declined 63% year-over-year, its payout ratio exceeds 7,000%, and the vast majority of what it 'pays' you is your own money being returned as capital destruction.

faqs.a: A yield trap is an investment that advertises an unsustainably high dividend yield. The high yield attracts income-seeking investors, but the underlying asset's value (NAV) declines faster than the distributions, resulting in negative total returns. The investor is essentially being paid back their own shrinking principal.

ULTY's 134% yield comes with extreme risks: distributions have declined 63% year-over-year, the payout ratio exceeds 7,000% (meaning it pays out far more than it earns), and most distributions are classified as return of capital. It may have a role in active traders' strategies, but it is not suitable for passive income investors.

faqs.q: Why does ULTY's yield keep going up even as distributions decline?
faqs.a: Because the share price is falling faster than the distributions. Yield = annual distributions ÷ share price. If distributions drop 50% but the share price drops 60%, the yield actually increases — creating the illusion of a better deal when the investment is actually deteriorating.

faqs.q: What's the difference between ULTY and MSTY?
faqs.a: MSTY writes covered calls on a single stock (MicroStrategy), while ULTY writes covered calls on a basket of high-volatility stocks. ULTY's multi-asset approach was designed to diversify risk, but the declining market has hit all underlying positions simultaneously, causing even faster distribution collapse than single-stock funds.

sections.heading: The Math That Doesn't Work
paragraphs: ULTY pays roughly $0.43 per share per week — about $22.36 annually at the current run rate. On a $30 stock, that's a 74% annualized yield from recent distributions alone. The advertised 134% yield uses trailing 12-month data that includes the much higher payouts from earlier in 2025, when weekly distributions exceeded $1.00.

Here's the problem: in January 2025, ULTY paid $5.715 in a single monthly distribution. By March 2026, the weekly distributions total roughly $1.70 per month — a 70% decline. The headline yield number is based on the higher historical payouts, not the current reality. Investors buying today expecting 134% returns are buying a mirage.

sections.heading: How to Spot a Yield Trap
paragraphs: Three red flags that separate yield traps from legitimate high-yield investments. First, declining distributions: if quarterly or weekly payouts are consistently shrinking, the income stream is evaporating. ULTY's distributions have fallen every quarter since mid-2025.

Second, return of capital exceeding 50%: when most of a distribution is classified as ROC, the fund is paying you back your own money while the NAV erodes. Check the fund's 19a-1 notices for ROC percentages.

Third, payout ratio above 100%: ULTY's 7,003% payout ratio means it pays out 70x what it earns. This is only possible by depleting assets — it's mathematically unsustainable. For comparison, a healthy dividend ETF like SCHD has a 61% payout ratio.
Analyze ULTY's complete distribution history, declining payout trends, and NAV erosion data with our ULTY Dividend Analysis tool — including a comparison against MSTY and TSLY.

sections.heading: The Alternative: Quality Over Yield
paragraphs: If 134% yields were real and sustainable, every pension fund in the world would own ULTY. They don't. Instead, institutional investors focus on dividend quality — companies and funds that can sustainably pay and grow their distributions over time.

SCHD yields 3.47% but has grown its dividend every year for over a decade. Realty Income yields 5% and has raised its monthly dividend 134 consecutive times. These aren't flashy numbers, but they're real — backed by actual earnings, not option premium harvesting from a depleting asset base.
Compare ULTY against sustainable income alternatives using our SCHD analysis and monthly dividend stocks comparison.
